questions.question_text: Under Idaho law, what must all real estate advertising include?
distractors: The individual agent's license number
The IREC logo and approval number
The agent's personal phone number
questions.explanation: Section 54-2053 requires all advertising of listed property to clearly and conspicuously contain the brokerage's licensed business name. This ensures consumers can identify the responsible brokerage behind any advertising.

questions.question_text: A salesperson places a 'For Sale' sign on a listing without the brokerage name. What Idaho law has been violated?
distractors: The Property Condition Disclosure Act
The Idaho Consumer Protection Act
The Local Land Use Planning Act
questions.explanation: Section 54-2053 requires all advertising of listed property, including signs, to display the brokerage's licensed business name. Omitting it is a violation of Idaho real estate advertising rules.

questions.question_text: An Idaho brokerage operates under the name 'Mountain View Realty LLC.' How must this appear in advertising?
questions.correct_answer: As the licensed business name registered with IREC
distractors: As any shortened version the broker prefers
Only as the DBA name on the business card
Using only the broker's personal name
questions.explanation: Advertising must use the brokerage's licensed business name as registered with IREC. Shortened or informal versions that differ from the registered name would violate advertising requirements.

questions.question_text: A newspaper ad for a listing shows only the agent's name and phone number. Is this compliant with Idaho law?
questions.correct_answer: No, the brokerage name must be included
distractors: Yes, newspaper ads are exempt
Yes, if the agent's license number is shown
No, but only if the ad is for a residential property
questions.explanation: All advertising must include the brokerage's licensed business name regardless of the medium. An ad showing only the agent's name without the brokerage name violates Section 54-2053.

questions.question_text: Which statement about Idaho real estate advertising is correct?
questions.correct_answer: The brokerage name must appear in all forms of advertising
distractors: Only print ads require the brokerage name
Social media posts are exempt from ad rules
The agent's name can substitute for the brokerage name
questions.explanation: Idaho advertising rules apply to all forms of advertising — print, digital, social media, signs, and internet. The brokerage's licensed business name must always be included.

questions.question_text: A team of agents wants to advertise under the name 'Idaho Dream Team.' What additional requirement must be met?
questions.correct_answer: The brokerage name must also appear in the ad
distractors: The team must register separately with IREC
Each team member's license number must appear
Team names are not permitted in Idaho
questions.explanation: Team or group names must include or be accompanied by the brokerage's licensed business name in all advertising. The team name alone is insufficient.

questions.question_text: A licensee posts a property listing on their personal social media page. What requirement applies?
questions.correct_answer: It must be identifiable as licensed brokerage activity
distractors: Social media is exempt from advertising rules
Only the agent's name is required
No rules apply to personal accounts
questions.explanation: Internet and social media advertising must be clearly identifiable as licensed brokerage activity. This means the brokerage name must be included even on personal social media accounts when advertising listings.

questions.question_text: Which statement about team advertising in Idaho is TRUE?
questions.correct_answer: Team names must include or accompany the brokerage name
distractors: Teams may advertise independently of the brokerage
Team names must be registered with DOPL separately
Only the team leader's name is required in ads
questions.explanation: IREC Guideline 13, interpreting the advertising statute, requires the full licensed brokerage name in every ad — a team name alone is not sufficient and is considered misleading advertising. Teams cannot advertise as though they are independent brokerages.

questions.question_text: An Idaho agent creates a website for their team that does not mention the brokerage. Is this a violation?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, internet advertising must include the brokerage name
distractors: No, websites are not considered advertising
No, if the brokerage name is on a separate page
Yes, but only if the site accepts leads
questions.explanation: All internet advertising, including websites, must be clearly identifiable as licensed brokerage activity and include the brokerage's licensed business name.

questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of requiring the brokerage name in all Idaho real estate advertising?
questions.correct_answer: To identify the responsible brokerage to consumers
distractors: To increase brokerage brand recognition
To track advertising expenditures for tax purposes
To ensure IREC can collect advertising fees
questions.explanation: The requirement ensures consumers can identify the responsible licensed brokerage behind any advertisement. This protects the public by providing accountability and a point of contact.

questions.question_text: An Idaho licensee wants to sell their own investment property. What disclosure is required?
questions.correct_answer: They must disclose their licensee status
distractors: No disclosure is needed for personal property
They must hire another agent to sell it
They must get IREC approval first
questions.explanation: Section 54-2055 requires licensees dealing in their own property to disclose their licensee status in writing, no later than presentation of the purchase and sale agreement. This ensures buyers know they are dealing with a licensed professional who has specialized knowledge.

questions.question_text: A licensed agent buys a fixer-upper for personal investment, then resells it without ever disclosing in writing to the buyer that they hold an active Idaho license. What rule was violated?
questions.correct_answer: The agent must disclose licensee status per Section 54-2055
distractors: No violation occurred for personal property sales
Only the purchase required disclosure, not the sale
This is a trust account violation
questions.explanation: Section 54-2055 requires a licensee dealing in their own property to disclose in writing, no later than presentation of the purchase and sale agreement, that they hold an active Idaho real estate license. It applies to both buying and selling, and the transaction must be conducted through the licensee's broker.

questions.question_text: Why does Idaho require licensees to disclose their status when buying or selling their own property?
questions.correct_answer: Licensees have specialized knowledge that creates an advantage
distractors: To allow IREC to collect transaction fees
To ensure proper tax reporting
To prevent licensees from owning property
questions.explanation: Licensed agents have professional knowledge about market values, negotiation, and transaction processes. Disclosure ensures the other party knows they are dealing with someone who has this specialized expertise.

questions.question_text: A licensee purchases a home for their family to live in. Must they disclose their license status to the seller?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, disclosure is required in all personal deals
distractors: No, primary residences are exempt
Only if using their brokerage to process it
Only if the purchase price exceeds $500,000
questions.explanation: Idaho law requires licensees to disclose their status in writing whenever dealing in their own property, whether it's an investment property or a personal residence. There are no exemptions based on property type or price.

questions.question_text: Which Idaho statute specifically requires licensees to disclose their status when dealing in their own property?
distractors: Section 54-2053
Section 54-2045
Section 54-2082
questions.explanation: Section 54-2055 requires licensees to disclose their licensee status when dealing in their own property. Section 54-2053 covers advertising, 54-2045 covers trust accounts, and 54-2082 covers agency relationships.
